data.title: Prospera Energy Acquires White Tundra Petroleum, Reaches Agreement on Convertible Debt
data.description:
Prospera Energy said overnight Thursday that it will acquire White Tundra Petroleum, which has oil-producing assets in Alberta.

Prospera will pay with 18 million newly issued shares, contingent upon White Tundra achieving 85 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d) for three consecutive days across its properties.

Prospera will also issue a performance-based bonus of 7,312,500 additional shares if production of 128 boe/d can be demonstrated for at least seven consecutive days within six months from the acquisition date.

Prospera is also assuming $695,000 in debt as part of the transaction.

Prospera also said it reached a settlement agreement with its convertible debt holders to address the upcoming maturity of its $1.5 million convertible debt, along with accrued interest of $559,374.82 as of the note maturity date on March 26.

Under the terms, the $1.5 million principal will be refinanced through the issuance of a 12-month promissory note bearing 12% interest, with monthly principal repayments of $250,000 commencing six months after issuance.

$200,000 of outstanding interest will be settled through a 12-month convertible note at 12% interest, convertible into Prospera shares at $0.05 apiece. The remainder will be settled through a shares-for-debt agreement at $0.04 per share.

Meanwhile, Prospera said its production continues to increase, exiting February at 878 boe/d, rising 10% from the previously reported February peak production.

On March 3, the company said Luseland production reached 130 boe/d, the highest since December 2023, while Hearts Hill achieved 208 boe/d, the highest since November 2019.

data.title: Gold heads for best week in six, US payrolls data on tap
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bullion has gained over 2% so far this week
  • Fed's Powell due to speak later in the day
  • Dollar heads for worst week since November 4

Gold prices climbed on Friday and were headed for their best week in six, buoyed by trade war concerns and a weaker dollar, while the market's focus shifted to the U.S. non-farm payrolls report due later in the day.

Spot gold added 0.3% to $2,919.19 an ounce as of 1214 GMT. Safe-haven bullion has gained over 2% so far this week, its best since the week of January 20, as U.S. President Donald Trump's ever-shifting tariff policies fanned uncertainty.

U.S. gold futures were unchanged at $2,926.40.

The U.S. dollar index is on course for its worst weekly performance since November 7, 2022, making greenback-priced bullion less expensive for foreign buyers.

Weakening in the dollar index, tariff concerns, and poor numbers from the U.S. economy, all these three factors have supported gold this week, said Ajay Kedia, director at Mumbai-based Kedia Commodities.

"I'm expecting some kind of consolidation or weakness in gold prices to the tune of around $2,872, acting as near term support unless we don't have any strong news," Kedia said.

Data earlier this week showed a slowdown in U.S. private payrolls growth in February, while U.S. jobless claims fell more than expected last week.

The U.S. non-farm payrolls report is due at 1330 GMT, and might offer cues to the Federal Reserve's monetary policy and will be followed by Fed Chair Jerome Powell's speech on the economic outlook.

"Weak payrolls could drive markets to believe in earlier rate cuts, aiding gold higher," said Nitesh Shah, commodities strategist at WisdomTree.

The Fed has held interest rates steady so far this year after executing three rate cuts last year, but the market expects easing to resume in June. (FEDWATCH)

Despite being an inflation hedge, higher interest rates may dampen the non-yielding asset's appeal.

Spot silver fell 0.5% to $32.47 an ounce and platinum shed 0.2% to $965.15, while palladium edged 0.3% up to $945.09.

data.description: ModivCare has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $9.95, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Nayax Raises $137 Million in Notes, Warrants Offering in Israel
data.description:
Nayax said Friday raised 496.5 million new Israeli shekels ($137.1 million) through the sale of 486,291 units to classified investors in Israel priced at 1,021 new Israeli shekels per unit.

Each unit consisted of 1,000 new Israeli shekels principle amount of notes and three warrants, the company said.

Net proceeds of around 486.3 million new Israeli shekels will be used for debt repayment, acquisitions, and general corporate purposes, the company said.

data.title: Gannett Prepays $57.5 Million on Term Loan Facility
data.description:
Gannett said Friday it prepaid about $57.5 million on its five-year first lien term loan facility.

In Q1, the company has reduced its first lien debt to $792.5 million from $850 million.

Gannett is well placed to further improve its capital structure this year and expects to repay "well over" $100 million of debt via asset sales and continued free cash flow growth, Chief Executive Michael Reed said.

data.description:

data.description: JPMorgan on Friday downgraded British hotel and restaurant operator Whitbread to neutral from overweight and reduced its price target to 30 pounds sterling from 44 pounds.

data.title: Hengdeli Holdings Flags Loss in 2024
data.description:
Hengdeli Holdings expects a minimum loss attributable to its equity shareholders of 65 million yuan for 2024, against an attributable profit of 33.9 million yuan in 2023, a Friday bourse filing said.

The consumer goods trader attributed the expected swing to a decrease in sales and gross profit, an increase in depreciation expenses on property, plant and equipment, and foreign exchange losses resulting from the exchange rate fluctuations.

data.title: This solid market indicator says to buy stocks now and enjoy double-digit gains
data.description:
By Mark Hulbert

The gold/platinum ratio has an impressive record. It's turned bullish on U.S. equities.

An obscure indicator with an excellent track record is forecasting a strong U.S. stock market over the next year.

This would be good news any time, but especially now with investors' mood so pessimistic. Fear has grown so extreme in some pockets on Wall Street that some even are now arguing that contrarian analysis has stopped working.

The indicator to which I refer is the ratio of gold's (GC00) price to platinum's (PL00). According to a 2019 study, "Gold, Platinum and Expected Stock Returns," the U.S. stock market more often than not rises over the subsequent 12 months when the gold/platinum ratio is in a strong uptrend - and vice versa. In fact, researchers Darien Huang, a former finance professor at Cornell University, and Mete Kilic, a finance professor at the University of Southern California, found that the ratio has a better track record than most of the other better-known indicators to which investors typically pay attention.

This is good news since the gold/platinum ratio has risen strongly over the last 12 months, as you can see from the chart above. The correlation between this ratio's trailing 12-month change and the stock market's subsequent 12-month change is significant at the 99% confidence level - higher even than the 95% level that statisticians often use when assessing whether a pattern is genuine.

The gold/platinum ratio has grown 25% over the past year, higher than 82% of all comparable readings of the past 20 years. As you can see from the table below, this puts the current ratio solidly in the quartile associated with impressive double-digit stock-market returns over the subsequent 12 months.

A solid stock-market indicator

The reason the gold/platinum ratio is a good stock market predictor, according to the study's authors, is that it's a proxy for geopolitical risk. On the one hand, both gold and silver (SI00) respond to changes in the economy's growth prospects, since both have industrial uses. But gold is far more sensitive than platinum to geopolitical risk. When the gold/platinum ratio is rising, therefore, it means geopolitical risk is rising independently of economic risk.

You may wonder why a rising ratio is good news for stock market investors, since increasing geopolitical risk is not something that investors celebrate.

To answer, it's important to distinguish between the gold/platinum ratio as a coincident indicator and as a leading indicator. As the former, equities do tend to be below-average performers when the ratio is rising. But a different picture emerges when measuring the ratio's record as a leading indicator: Rising geopolitical risk eventually declines, and when it does, equities tend to be above-average performers.

To be sure, despite the gold/platinum ratio rising strongly over the past year, the U.S. stock market also has been quite strong - with the S&P 500 SPX producing a total return of 16.5%. But if the researchers' theory is right, that return would have been even higher except for the heightened geopolitical risk. Some of that return has been shifted into the future, which is why the ratio's message is now as bullish as it is.

Mark Hulbert is a regular contributor to MarketWatch. His Hulbert Ratings tracks investment newsletters that pay a flat fee to be audited. He can be reached at mark@hulbertratings.com

More: Worried about the stock selloff? These low-volatility ETFs could help you manage the market.

Also read: These positive stock-market indicators could rally the S&P 500 above 6,000

-Mark Hulbert

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Primaris REIT Gets TSX Green Light to Buy Back 10% of Public Float
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Primaris Real Estate Investment Trust plans to renew its share repurchase program to buy back up to 10% of its public float over the course of a year.

The Canadian REIT said Friday that it has received approval by the Toronto Stock Exchange to buy back up to 7.6 million series A units on the open market.

Shares have been virtually flat year-to-date, closing Thursday at 15.45 Canadian dollars ($10.81). Over the past 52-weeks, the stock is up about 13%.

At Thursday's closing price, the value of the shares to be bought back would be worth around C$116.9 million.

Primaris can begin to buy back shares starting on March 11.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: UK Health Regulator Approves Vertex's Cystic Fibrosis Drug Alyftrek
data.description:
The UK's Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency said Friday it approved Vertex Pharmaceuticals' Alyftrek for treating cystic fibrosis, a genetic condition characterized by mucus buildup in the lungs and digestive system.

The marketing authorization covers the use of once-daily oral drug Alyftrek in patients six years and above with specific mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator gene, including F508del.

Two late-stage studies showed that Alyftrek worked as well as the currently approved Kaftrio in improving the lung function and reducing sweat chloride levels of patients.

data.title: Bayer seeks investor approval for 35% cash call to gird for litigation
data.description:
Bayer will seek shareholder approval to raise equity capital worth close to 35% of its outstanding shares over the next three years to cover possible costs of U.S. litigation, it said on Friday, knocking its shares lower.

The capital increase would be worth about 8.4 billion euros ($9.1 billion) based on the company's current market value.

It will be voted on at the annual general meeting on April 25 and will only be used "if it is absolutely necessary," Bayer said in a statement on its website.

But it said it could not rule out having to increase capital at short notice in the future, for example "in view of potential future settlements with plaintiffs in the United States, or in connection with measures to substantially contain litigations in the United States".

Bayer shares were down 7.6% at 1115 GMT.

Analysts had told Reuters last year that Bayer may have to ask shareholders for capital to shore up its finances even after the German drugs and pesticides maker cut dividends.

CEO Bill Anderson has struggled to revive a share price that has plunged by more than 70% since Bayer's $63 billion acquisition of Monsanto in 2018 that saddled it with costly litigation and debt.

The company's problems include U.S. litigation alleging harm from weedkiller glyphosate, a 2023 development setback for its most promising experimental medicine, weak agriculture markets and pressure from some investors to separate or sell businesses.

It has paid about $10 billion to settle disputed claims that Roundup, based on the herbicide glyphosate, causes cancer. About 67,000 further cases are pending for which the group has set aside $5.9 billion in legal provisions.

Bayer said stockholders would be granted subscription rights in the event of a cash call.

($1 = 0.9222 euros)

data.title: Morgan Stanley Raises Price Target on Broadcom to $260 From $246, Keeps Overweight Rating
data.description: Broadcom has an average rating of overweight and mean price target of $253.24, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Mullen Group Announced Renewal of Normal Course Issuer Bid for Shares and Approval of Normal Course Issuer Bid for Debentures
data.description:
Mullen Group announced Friday it has received approval from Toronto Stock Exchange for renewal of its normal course issuer bid on common shares and approval to commence a normal course issuer bid for 5.75% Convertible Unsecured Subordinated Debentures.

For its normal course issuer bid for the shares that began on March 11, 2024, and that will expire on March 10, 2025, Mullen previously received approval from the TSX to repurchase up to 8,220,349 shares. Of this amount, as of February 28, 2025, the corporation had repurchased 506,380 shares for a total cost of approximately $6.64 million, representing a volume weighted average price of $13.11 per share.

Pursuant to the bid, the corporation may repurchase from time to time up to a maximum of 8,157,012 shares, which represents, as at February 28, 2025, approximately 10% of the public float. Additionally, pursuant to the bid, the corporation may repurchase from time to time up to a maximum of $12,015,000 principal amount of debentures, representing 10% of the Public Float of the debentures. The Bid shall commence on March 11, 2025, and expire at the closing of trading on March 10, 2026.

data.title: Ultralife Corp expected to post earnings of 15 cents a share - Earnings Preview
data.description:
• Ultralife Corp is expected to show a fall in quarterly revenue when it reports results on March 11 for the period ending December 31 2024

  • The Newark New York New York-based company is expected to report a 10.2% decrease in revenue to $40 million from $44.55 million a year ago, according to the estimate from one analyst, based on LSEG data.

  • ​LSEG's mean analyst estimate for Ultralife Corp is for earnings of 15 cents per share.

  • The one available analyst rating on the shares is "buy".

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts was unchanged in the last three months. ​

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Ultralife Corp is $14.00​, above​ its last closing price of $6.01. ​​​
